frameworks.name: resource-rational analysis
frameworks.one_line: Brain minimizes weighted sum of behavioral and neural encoding costs

frameworks.name: normative theory of working memory
frameworks.one_line: Set size effects derived from optimal cost minimization

frameworks.name: fixed-capacity working memory
frameworks.one_line: Hard-limit accounts that treat capacity as a constraint

core_question: Can set size effects in visual working memory be explained by rational minimization of behavioral and neural costs rather than fixed capacity limits?
key_assumptions: Encoding precision incurs a neural cost
Behavioral cost is error-based on recall
Observer trades off these costs to set precision
Probing probability modulates expected behavioral cost
manipulations: Set size varied across delayed-estimation tasks
Probing probability varied across items or conditions
Model fit across nine delayed-estimation experiments
key_findings: Model predicts decreasing encoding precision with set size
Set size effects are mediated by probing probability
Total invested resource can vary non-monotonically with set size
Sometimes optimal to encode only a subset or none of the items
interpretations: Cognitive limitations may reflect rational cost minimization rather than hard capacity constraints
Set size effects emerge as optimal resource allocation given encoding costs
